skill.name: convex-performance-audit
skill.description: Audits Convex application performance by tracing hot reads, write contention, subscription cost, and function budget limits.
skill.summary: Find and prioritize practical fixes for slow or expensive Convex app paths.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review found they are mostly documentation examples, Markdown code fences, TypeScript API names, numeric limits, and external reference links. The confirmed risk is legitimate command guidance that may run the Convex CLI through npx and contact Convex services, so publication is acceptable with a command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: External Convex CLI Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Convex CLI insight commands, including an npx fallback that can download the latest package and contact Convex services. This is appropriate for the skill purpose, but it should be user-approved because it executes external tooling and may use local project credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command text is explicit and semantically tied to performance diagnostics. It is legitimate operational guidance, but it creates real execution and network exposure if run without user consent.

low_findings.title: External Documentation and Package Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to Convex documentation and the npm page for the aggregate component. These are normal references, but they are network touchpoints outside the local workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links to relevant vendor and package documentation. No evidence found that they exfiltrate data or are used by executable code.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command, SQLite, system reconnaissance, and weak cryptography alerts come from Markdown examples, Convex API names, numeric limits, and prose. No evidence found of hidden scripts, prompt injection, credential theft, or malicious execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed the cited material is non-executing Markdown or YAML content. The suspicious terms appear in educational TypeScript snippets and descriptions, not in runnable skill scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow suggests running npx-based Convex diagnostics. Agents should ask before executing it and should explain that it may download packages and access deployment telemetry.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present in the skill instructions. The risk is bounded because it supports the stated Convex audit purpose and is not paired with suspicious data handling.

content.value_statement: Slow Convex apps often hide cost in broad reads, reactive subscriptions, and write contention. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a focused audit and practical fixes.
actual_capabilities: Routes Convex performance symptoms to focused reference guidance.
Uses insights, dashboard signals, or code review to scope one user flow.
Traces reads, writes, subscriptions, and sibling functions that share tables.
Recommends fixes for indexes, digest tables, batching, and no-op writes.
Checks rollout safety when denormalized fields or indexes need migration.
limitations: Requires access to the Convex project code for code-based audits.
Runtime insights may require Convex CLI access and deployment permissions.
Does not replace load testing or production observability.
Large schema or migration changes still require human review.

use_cases.title: Diagnose a Slow Product Page
use_cases.description: Trace queries, joins, and subscriptions for one slow Convex user flow and identify the highest-impact fix.

use_cases.title: Reduce Deployment Cost Signals
use_cases.description: Review high bytes read, high documents scanned, or expensive reactive subscriptions from Convex insights.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Write-Heavy Features
use_cases.description: Find OCC conflict sources, hot documents, and unnecessary writes that increase latency under load.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Convex Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Start with a slow page or feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Convex feature for performance issues. Focus on one user flow, trace its reads and writes, and suggest the smallest useful fix.

prompt_templates.title: Use Insights Output
prompt_templates.scenario: You have Convex CLI or dashboard findings.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use these Convex insights results to identify the problem class. Prioritize high bytes read, documents scanned, OCC conflicts, and subscription cost.

prompt_templates.title: Subscription Cost Review
prompt_templates.scenario: A page has many live updates or re-renders.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this page for excessive Convex subscriptions and invalidation cost. Explain which queries should stay reactive and which can use point-in-time reads.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Sibling Function Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: A fix may affect multiple functions or tables.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit sibling readers and writers that touch these Convex tables. Check index rollout safety, denormalized fallback behavior, no-op writes, and migration risks.

output_examples.input: A dashboard query reads thousands of project documents and filters in JavaScript.
output_examples.output: The audit identifies the broad scan as the hot path, recommends a storage index, and asks to verify sibling list queries.

output_examples.input: A page renders many cards, and each card runs its own user lookup query.
output_examples.output: The audit recommends batching related data into a parent query or using denormalized display fields when the flow is hot.

output_examples.input: Mutations retry often when users update the same shared record.
output_examples.output: The audit points to hot document contention, suggests splitting the write target, and checks for no-op patches.

best_practices: Start with measured signals when available, then audit code paths when signals are missing.
Fix sibling functions that touch the same tables so performance behavior stays consistent.
Verify correctness and fallback behavior before switching hot paths to new indexes or digest data.
anti_patterns: Do not introduce digest tables or denormalization without a measured hot path or clear growth risk.
Do not treat every reactive query as bad; keep live updates where product freshness matters.
Do not run Convex CLI commands against deployments without user approval and the right environment.

faq.question: What does this skill audit?
faq.answer: It audits Convex reads, writes, subscriptions, OCC conflicts, function limits, and related performance tradeoffs.

faq.question: Does it need Convex insights output?
faq.answer: Insights output helps, but the skill can still audit code when runtime signals are unavailable.

faq.question: Can it change application code?
faq.answer: It can guide code changes, but larger migrations or cross-cutting fixes should be reviewed before editing.

faq.question: Why does it mention npx commands?
faq.answer: The commands collect Convex performance signals. They should be run only with user approval.

faq.question: Is this only for backend functions?
faq.answer: No. It also reviews client subscription patterns, pagination, and UI update cost.

faq.question: Does it guarantee lower Convex costs?
faq.answer: No. It identifies likely cost drivers and verification steps, but results depend on traffic and data shape.
